filer: CHAMBER OF COMMERCE OF THE UNITED STATES
source_text: sources/comments/files/20251121-5044__CHAMBER-OF-COMMERCE-OF-THE-UNITED-STATES/FINAL USCC Comments to FERC on DOE Large Load Interconnection ANOPR.txt
overall_summary: The Chamber generally supports FERC's effort to streamline large load interconnection but warns that reforms must not upend state-level progress or delay pending requests. It asks the Commission to raise the large load threshold to 75 to 100 MW, allocate costs transparently and in proportion to benefits so other customers are not harmed, add study deposits and withdrawal penalties, keep hybrid configurations voluntary, finalize the pending colocation proceeding, and preserve regional flexibility.

quotes.text: The Chamber is generally supportive of the Commission's efforts, in part due to the Department of Energy's entreaty, to explore reforms that have the potential to enhance the interconnection process for large loads.
quotes.concern: General support for FERC exploring large load interconnection reforms

quotes.text: the preexisting process, handled primarily on a state-level basis, is not broken everywhere. Many large loads have been and continue to be connected at the transmission level nationwide. Thus, any reforms proposed or implemented because of this proceeding should be mindful to not upend the progress that is currently being made to interconnect large loads across the country; new delays should not inadvertently be imposed by reforms intended to achieve the opposite result.
quotes.concern: Support is conditional on not disrupting working state-level processes

quotes.text: Streamlined Interconnection Processes: The Commission should prioritize reforms that reduce delays and administrative burdens in the interconnection process, including through the potential adoption of AI technologies to support the automation and acceleration of required studies, consistent with prudent utility practice.
quotes.concern: Streamline and automate interconnection studies to cut delays

quotes.text: Cost Allocation Transparency: It is essential that the costs associated with interconnecting large loads are allocated in a fair and transparent manner. Clear cost allocation methodologies will provide certainty to large load customers and generation and transmission developers and ensure that costs are equitably allocated, as appropriate, across relevant stakeholder constituencies.
quotes.concern: Cost allocation should be fair, transparent, and methodologically clear

quotes.text: Grid Reliability and Resilience: The integration of large loads must not compromise grid reliability or resilience. The Commission should ensure the grid can accommodate these loads without adverse impacts on existing customers or the overall integrity of the grid.
quotes.concern: Large load integration must not harm reliability or existing customers

quotes.text: Support for Innovation: The interconnection process should encourage the adoption of innovative technologies and practices, such as expanded use of voluntary demand response programs, energy storage, hybrid facilities, and advanced grid management systems, provided that such options are amenable to all directly impacted entities, to optimize the integration of large loads.
quotes.concern: Encourage flexible options such as demand response, storage, and hybrids

quotes.text: the Commission should promptly finalize its pending proceeding regarding the colocation of generation with such large loads. A holistic approach is necessary
quotes.concern: Finalize the pending colocation proceeding for aligned policy

quotes.text: the Chamber recommends that the floor for “large load” interconnections be raised to 75 or 100 MW. An increased minimum threshold would better reflect the scale of modern large load projects, ensuring that regulatory frameworks are aligned with current and future industry needs.
quotes.concern: Raise the large load threshold from 20 MW to 75 to 100 MW

quotes.text: The ANOPR's fourth principle should be strengthened to include study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties that enhance the veracity and transparency of the load growth currently anticipated across the power grid.
quotes.concern: Add deposits, readiness rules, and penalties to deter speculative requests

quotes.text: Such hybrid options should be available on a voluntary basis when the sought “speed to power” for a large load is not otherwise available, and the simultaneous study of load and generation behind a single point of interconnection has merit.
quotes.concern: Keep hybrid generation-load configurations voluntary and temporary

quotes.text: large loads are committed to paying their full cost of service for the energy and infrastructure supporting the electric service they require. Ultimately, large load interconnections should result in lower, rather than elevated, electricity rates for all other customers. The Commission's efforts to streamline large load interconnections should ensure that costs are allocated commensurate with the benefits received from any needed network upgrades.
quotes.concern: Large loads pay full cost of service; allocate costs to benefits, no cost shift

quotes.text: the Commission should commit specific attention to ensuring that any new interconnection queues or processes not be developed or implemented in a way that unduly delays or interrupts pending requests.
quotes.concern: New processes must not delay or interrupt already-pending requests

quotes.text: flexibility should be provided – consistent with the final paragraph of the ANOPR – to ensure that equivalent or superior solutions to large load interconnection can be continued or newly developed on a utility-specific or larger regional bases, as appropriate.
quotes.concern: Preserve utility-specific and regional flexibility in solutions

bins.name: Streamline studies, protect pending requests
bins.description: The Chamber supports streamlining and automating interconnection studies to reduce delays, including through AI. It conditions that support on not upending working state-level processes and insists new queues must not delay requests already pending.

bins.name: Transparent cost allocation to beneficiaries
bins.description: Cost allocation should be fair, transparent, and tied to a clear methodology. Large loads should pay their full cost of service so that interconnections lower rather than raise rates for other customers, with costs assigned in proportion to benefits from upgrades.

bins.name: Finalize pending colocation proceeding
bins.description: The Chamber urges the Commission to promptly finalize its pending colocation proceeding so that generation-with-load policy is aligned and mutually reinforcing with this rulemaking.

bins.name: Regional flexibility and innovative options
bins.description: The Chamber wants the process to encourage voluntary options such as demand response, storage, and hybrids when stakeholders accept them, and to preserve utility-specific and regional flexibility so working approaches are not disrupted.

bins.name: Raise large load threshold to 75 to 100 MW
bins.description: The Chamber recommends raising the minimum large load threshold from the proposed 20 MW to 75 or 100 MW so grid operators focus on truly impactful interconnections and queues are not overwhelmed by smaller loads.

bins.name: Study deposits and withdrawal penalties
bins.description: The Chamber asks that the fourth principle be strengthened with study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties to deter duplicative and speculative requests and improve the accuracy of anticipated load growth.

bins.name: Expedite related colocation proceeding
bins.description: The Chamber wants the Commission to move quickly on its pending colocation proceeding so a holistic, coordinated approach across related rulemakings is in place.

bins.name: Voluntary, temporary hybrid configurations
bins.description: Hybrid load-and-generation configurations should be available on a voluntary basis to provide speed to power when full grid service is delayed, with simultaneous study of load and generation behind a single point of interconnection.

bins.name: Protect existing customers from harm
bins.description: The Chamber stresses that large loads must not compromise reliability or impose adverse impacts on existing customers, and that cost allocation should ensure interconnections lower rather than raise rates for everyone else.
